retrieved_docs.title: Allergy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis treatment begins with triage and the removal from exposure, which can involve decontamination if topical agents are involved or clothing is contaminated. Removal of the patient's clothing and standard precautions by staff such as masks, gloves, and gown should take place. Hand washing by staff who may have used latex gloves or eaten peanuts may be necessary. ABC’s (airway, breathing, and circulation) of emergency care should have priority.  Patients should have oxygen and intravenous administration of fluids. The patient should receive epinephrine intramuscularly (IM) (1 to 1000 dilution {1 mg/1ml} 0.3cc) 0.3 to 0.5 mg which can be repeated every 5 to 15 minutes as needed. Most patients should respond to 1 to 3 doses if symptoms persist, progress or the patient becomes hypotensive IV epinephrine should be started at 0.1 mcg/kg/minute by infusion device and titrated to maintain perfusion of body organs. Patients on beta-blockers should be given glucagon 1 to 5 mg over 5 minutes. Antiemetics should be given to treat vomiting often associated with glucagon administration. Histamine type 2 receptor blockers such as famotidine 20 mg IV or ranitidine at  50 mg IV should be administered. The glucocorticoid, methylprednisolone 125 mg, should be given. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Epinephrine
retrieved_docs.content: Epinephrine is given through intramuscular injection and at a dose of 0.3 to 0.5 mL of 1:1,000 concentration of epinephrine. Pediatric dosing is 0.01 mg/kg or 0.15 mg intramuscularly (epinephrine injection for pediatric dosage). Intramuscular delivery has proven to provide more rapid delivery and produce better outcomes than subcutaneous or intravascular. Note if intravenous epinephrine is to be given, the concentration required is 1:10,000; see the next paragraph. The thigh is preferred to the deltoid when possible. Repeat studies have shown that providers often wait too long before giving epinephrine; it is the treatment of choice, and the rapid benefit much outweighs the risks of withholding treatment. While most patients require only a single dose, repeat doses may be given every 5 to 10 minutes as needed until symptoms improve. If patients require multiple doses, a continuous infusion of epinephrine may be considered; start an initial intravenous infusion of 0.1 mg of 1:10,000, given over 5 to 10 minutes. If more is required, begin infusion at 1 microgram per minute and titrate to effect. Stop intravenous infusion if arrhythmia or chest pain develops. The risk of cardiovascular complications is much greater for intravenous epinephrine. For beta-blocked patients, close blood pressure monitoring is suggested due to the risk of unopposed alpha-adrenergic effects from epinephrine.

retrieved_docs.title: Epinephrine -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylactic shock: For anaphylactic shock in adults and children with a body weight of 30 kg (66 lbs) or more, the recommended dose of epinephrine is 0.3 to 0.5 mg (0.3-0.5 mL), administered intramuscularly or subcutaneously into the anterolateral aspect of the thigh every 5 to 10 minutes as needed. For managing anaphylactic shock, a 0.1 mg/mL solution of epinephrine may be administered via IV route. The typical initial dose is 0.1 mg (1 mL), given at a rate of 1 to 4 mcg/min.

retrieved_docs.title: Epinephrine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Epinephrine, a widely used medication and hormone, is critical in managing various medical conditions due to its potent action on the sympathetic nervous system. Epinephrine is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating type 1 hypersensitivity reactions (including anaphylaxis), managing hypotension from septic shock, and inducing mydriasis during intraocular surgeries. Epinephrine exerts its effects through α- and β-adrenergic receptors with dose-dependent actions as a sympathomimetic catecholamine. Low doses primarily activate β-receptors, enhancing bronchodilation and cardiac activity, while higher doses engage α-receptors to induce vasoconstriction and increase vascular tone. Adverse effects of the drug include tachycardia, hypertension, headache, anxiety, and tremors.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Epinephrine Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended dose of epinephrine is 0.01 mg/kg, up to a maximum of 0.5 mg, which may be repeated every 5 to 15 minutes as necessary. About 35% of patients require a second dose. In the United States, epinephrine ampules are now labeled as 1 mg/mL (formerly 1:1000). Autoinjectors are available in 0.1, 0.15, and 0.3 mg doses. When using autoinjectors or when there may be delays in weight-based dosing, the guidelines should be followed: Less than 10 kg: Use a 0.1 mg autoinjector or draw 0.1 mL of 1 mg/mL solution; 0.15 mg is acceptable if 0.1 mg is unavailable 10 to 25 kg: Use a 0.15 mg autoinjector or draw 0.15 mL 25 to 50 kg: Use a 0.3 mg autoinjector or draw 0.3 mL More than 50 kg: Draw 0.5 mL; if unavailable, use a 0.3 mg autoinjector [39] [40] [41] Experts define refractory anaphylaxis as persistent symptoms after 3 epinephrine doses and supportive care. These patients may require an IV epinephrine infusion:

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Epinephrine Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Adults: Start at 0.1 µg/kg/min, titrate every 2 to 3 minutes by 0.05 µg/kg/min; typical range: 0.05 to 0.2 µg/kg/min Pediatrics: 0.1 to 1 µg/kg/min

retrieved_docs.title: Epinephrine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for epinephrine use in anaphylaxis, septic shock, and intraocular surgeries. Implement safe administration practices to minimize the adverse effects of epinephrine, including accurate dosing and monitoring. Select the correct epinephrine dose and route of administration for various clinical scenarios. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare team members to manage emergencies requiring epinephrine, ensuring timely and effective care. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Vasopressors
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressors may be substituted when a patient requires more doses of epinephrine but have unacceptable side effects from the epinephrine intravenous infusion (arrhythmia or chest pain). If no clear second-line pressor is identified, treatment guidelines would follow those of any other patient in hypotensive shock.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening emergency that demands immediate action. If possible, remove the triggering agent right away. Administering intramuscular (IM) epinephrine into the lateral thigh without delay is the most critical step. Repeat IM or intravenous (IV) doses may be needed. Unless prevented by airway swelling, the patient should be placed in a supine position with legs elevated to support blood flow. For pregnant patients, the left lateral position is used to avoid compressing the vena cava. These positions help maintain organ perfusion and prevent sudden cardiovascular collapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- An Evidence-based Approach to Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis is a rapid systemic and unpredictable disorder that is life-threatening. The disorder often occurs after exposure to certain antigens, mostly insect venom, food, or medications. The condition is immune-mediated and rapidly induces symptoms. For those who have not encountered anaphylaxis, the diagnosis can be difficult. In the United States, the rates of anaphylaxis have doubled over the past 20 years, and at least 1500 people die from the disorder each year. The potentially life-threatening nature of the disorder makes it imperative that one be prepared and offer prompt treatment. This is 1 of the few medical disorders during which a healthcare worker does not have the luxury of time. Having epinephrine on hand can be life-saving if one wants to avoid a fatal outcome. However, despite considerable evidence that epinephrine is the drug of choice, it is under-used. Instead, many healthcare workers use an antihistamine, a drug that does not prevent or relieve all the manifestations of anaphylaxis. [20] In addition, the antihistamine does not even start to work for 1 to 3 hours, depending on the route of administration. Given these findings, all healthcare workers must be educated on anaphylaxis and its treatment. Evidence indicates that healthcare workers are critical for managing and preventing anaphylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Drug Reactions -- Clinical Significance -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis constitutes a medical emergency. The offending agent should be immediately stopped, and IM epinephrine should be administered promptly. [66] Simultaneous oxygen administration, fluid resuscitation, and albuterol nebulization should be provided. Adjunctive therapies include intravenous antihistamines (histamine 1 and 2 receptor antagonists) and intravenous corticosteroids. If there is evidence of impending airway obstruction or compromise, intubation is required. In the case of anaphylactic shock, the patient may require additional fluid and vasopressor administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Administration of Vaccines and Epinephrine Autoinjection -- Complications -- Anaphylaxis and Epinephrine Autoinjector
retrieved_docs.content: The patient may feel tremulous and experience palpitations; this is an expected side effect of epinephrine. At this point, make sure to call emergency services and have the patient moved to a hospital setting where physicians can monitor them. Emergency services must observe the patient in case of a biphasic anaphylactic reaction, a secondary anaphylactic crisis that occurs after the initial onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Combining an H1 antihistamine with an H2 antihistamine, such as famotidine, may help patients achieve a better reduction in urticaria symptoms. However, antihistamines should not be used as monotherapy, as they do not mitigate upper or lower airway obstruction, shock, or hypotension. Antihistamines have not been shown to affect hospitalization rates or epinephrine requirements. Some studies suggest that when H2 antagonists are given IV, they may increase rates of hypotension. The recommended dose of famotidine is 20 mg for adults and 0.25 mg/kg for children, up to a maximum dose of 20 mg.

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Administration of Vaccines and Epinephrine Autoinjection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Discussions on vaccine administration can be divided into three separate areas: patient history and consent, the immunization itself, and typical issues following vaccinations, including anaphylaxis. The purpose of this article is to help medical professionals safely and competently administer immunizations, as well as be comfortable with administering an epinephrine auto-injection in the case of anaphylaxis following inoculation.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: High-flow oxygen should be administered using a nonrebreather mask at 15 L/min, and IV access should be established promptly. For stable patients, IV fluids should be started at 125 mL/h of normal saline for adults and at weight-based maintenance rates for children. Intubation should be anticipated in the presence of any respiratory distress, stridor, voice changes, or visible airway swelling. For patients who remain hypotensive after epinephrine, clinicians should begin aggressive fluid resuscitation. Adults typically require 1 to 2 L of normal saline rapidly, whereas children require 20 mL/kg boluses over 5 to 10 min, repeated as needed. Lactated Ringer may lead to metabolic alkalosis; however, large volumes of normal saline can cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, so some experts choose to switch to lactated Ringer when large volumes of normal saline are necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Evaluation -- Clinical Criteria for Anaphylaxis (1 of the following with onset within minutes to hours)*
retrieved_docs.content: Known exposure to an antigen and hypotension (systolic less than 90 mm Hg or a decrease of greater than 30% from baseline). It is not required to meet these criteria to treat; it is only to serve as a guide for diagnosis.

In addition to its inclusion in these complex mixtures, adrenaline is recognized as a treatment for septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة), reflecting its critical role in acute management of severe infection‑induced circulatory failure. The convergence of adrenaline’s pharmacologic activity in both multi‑ingredient oral preparations and emergency therapy underscores its importance in supporting cardiovascular stability and systemic physiological responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1849
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa is classified as a centrally acting antiadrenergic agent and is a member of the methyldopa drug class. Use of methyldopa is contraindicated in patients who have a hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس) reaction to the medication.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate that epinephrine serves both as a standard therapeutic agent in injectable form and as a component of diverse alternative‑medicine preparations aimed at supporting endocrine, nervous‑system, and metabolic functions.

retrieved_docs.content: Diphenhydramine hydrochloride, an antihistamine commonly used for allergic reactions, is contraindicated in حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (pregnancy), indicating that it should be avoided in pregnant patients because its use may pose risks to the developing fetus.

This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

The presence of adrenalin alongside other hormones and neurotransmitters reflects its role within the adaptive hormone system, as indicated by the broader relationship between adaptive hormones and adrenalin. Additionally, adrenalin is identified as the ingredient in the epinephrine 1 MG/ML injectable solution, underscoring its use as a pharmacologically active catecholamine across both human and veterinary therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1453
retrieved_docs.content: plant seeds and flowers). These ancillary ingredients are likely intended to supply nutritional support, antioxidant capacity, and additional pharmacologic actions that complement the primary adrenergic and corticotropic effects. The repeated pattern of pairing epinephrine with corticotropin, together with other endocrine hormones, neurotransmitters, minerals, vitamins, and herbal components, suggests a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating sympathetic activity, adrenal cortical output, thyroid function, and overall metabolic homeostasis. The inclusion of both synthetic and natural substances across the products underscores a multimodal approach to patient (or animal) care that leverages synergistic actions of catecholamines, ACTH, and supportive nutrients to address conditions such as allergic reactions, neuro‑behavioral imbalances, weight management, and systemic endocrine deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 461
retrieved_docs.content: (131i).” This receptor interaction underlies the classic catecholamine actions of adrenalin, such as increasing heart rate, bronchodilation, and metabolic mobilization. Moreover, adrenalin is classified within the broader category of adaptive hormones, indicating its participation in the body’s stress‑response and homeostatic adaptation mechanisms. Together, these relationships illustrate that adrenalin functions both as a standalone therapeutic agent (e.g., in injectable epinephrine solutions) and as a component of multi‑ingredient products designed to modulate endocrine, nervous, and metabolic systems via adrenergic receptor activation. Understanding adrenalin’s inclusion in such formulations and its receptor‑mediated actions is essential for clinicians and pharmacists when evaluating therapeutic intent, potential drug interactions, and overall patient or animal care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: antagonist distinct from the other H1‑blocking agents. Overall, the taxonomy illustrates how individual drugs—ranging from antiallergic nasal agents, plain and decongestant sympathomimetics, adrenergic substances, H1‑histamine antagonists, to specialized therapies for hereditary angioedema and rare musculoskeletal disorders—are organized into overlapping classes that guide precise therapeutic selection and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 78
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenalin (epinephrine) is a catecholamine hormone synthesized by the adrenal medulla and classified within the adrenal and gonadal hormone family as an adrenergic substance. It functions as an agonist at both α‑adrenergic and β‑adrenergic receptors, placing it in the sympathomimetic class and linking it to adaptive, stress‑response pathways that regulate cardiovascular tone, bronchodilation, fluid balance and metabolic activity. Clinically, adrenalin is employed for emergency cardiovascular support, anaphylaxis, bronchodilation and septic shock, and appears as an active ingredient in numerous injectable and oral liquid formulations (e.g., 1 ML epinephrine 1 MG/ML injection, Neurobalance Pro, OHM Pituitarum Complex, Appetite and Weight Natural Pet, FCS HP, MBM 11 Thyroid Adrenal, HP Axis) as well as specialized veterinary products. Metabolically, adrenalin is converted to normetanefrin, a catecholamine‑derived tumor biomarker, and is mapped to dipivefrin, a sympathomimetic used in

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1367
retrieved_docs.content: Skopolamin produces a physiological effect of decreasing histamine activity, and this reduction in histamine activity can modulate histamine‑mediated processes such as allergic reactions and gastric acid secretion, contributing to the drug’s overall therapeutic profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylát, and etodolac are each contraindicated in the presence of فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). This shared contraindication underscores the necessity of evaluating a patient’s allergy history before prescribing any of these medications, as exposure in individuals with drug hypersensitivity could precipitate severe allergic reactions. Proper assessment and avoidance of these agents in susceptible patients are essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 0 -- Community 8
retrieved_docs.content: clinicians with a structured framework for selecting appropriate bronchodilators, systemic adrenergics, mineralocorticoids and diagnostic tools based on receptor selectivity, route of administration, therapeutic group, contraindications and combination formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1355
retrieved_docs.content: Alglucerase, atrakurium, teriparatide, etodolac, famotidine, and sodium selenate are all medications that are contraindicated in the presence of drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). For patients who have a known hypersensitivity to drugs, the use of any of these agents should be avoided to prevent allergic reactions and potential adverse outcomes. Recognizing drug hypersensitivity as a contraindication for these specific drugs is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylate, triptorelin, etodolac, and carteolol are all medications that should be avoided in individuals with drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). In patients who exhibit an allergic or hypersensitivity reaction to drugs, the use of any of these agents is contraindicated because they may precipitate or exacerbate the hypersensitivity response. Recognizing drug hypersensitivity as a contraindicating condition for these specific drugs is essential for safe prescribing practices and for preventing adverse drug reactions in clinical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenalin, also known as epinephrine, is the principal active ingredient in several pharmaceutical preparations. It is the key component of the 1 mL epinephrine 1 mg/mL Injectable Solution, as well as of the oral liquid formulations identified as the OHM Pituitarum Complex (which contains EPINEPHRINE 10 [hp_X] among many other hormonal and tissue extracts) and the HP Axis oral liquid (which lists EPINEPHRINE 8 [hp_X] together with a wide array of mineral, botanical, and hormonal substances). In addition to its role as a drug ingredient, adrenalin is classified among adaptive hormones, reflecting its function in the body’s rapid stress‑response mechanisms. Its physiological actions are mediated through adrenergní receptory (adrenergic receptors), which transmit the catecholamine’s effects on cardiovascular, respiratory, and metabolic systems. Together, these relationships underscore adrenalin’s importance both as a therapeutic agent in emergency and hormonal preparations and as a central mediator of the body’s adaptive stress response.
